episodes.videoTitle: The AI PM playbook — with Jane Doe
episodes.summary: Jane Doe argues the PM role is being rewritten by AI: less spec-writing, more judgment about what good output looks like. The throughline is that taste and evaluation become the scarce skills.

insights.headline: AI shifts PMs from spec-writers to taste-makers
insights.detail: As AI drafts specs and flows, the PM's edge moves from producing the artifact to judging it. Jane cites her team cutting spec-writing time 60% but doubling time spent defining 'good', because the model will produce something either way and the real bottleneck becomes taste.
insights.anchorQuote: the bottleneck is taste, not typing

insights.headline: Write eval suites before building the AI feature
insights.detail: Jane's team writes the evaluation set first — 50 to 100 graded examples — before building an AI feature, so quality is measurable from day one. She says teams that skip this ship demos that work 70% of the time and never close the last 30%.
insights.anchorQuote: we write the evals before the feature

insights.headline: On product strategy
insights.detail: They discussed product strategy and how it matters for various teams in many situations.

episodes.videoTitle: Agents in production — with John Roe
episodes.summary: John Roe on why most agent projects stall: capability is no longer the hard part, reliability is. The conversation centers on the engineering of determinism and error recovery.

insights.headline: Agent reliability, not capability, is the 2026 bottleneck
insights.detail: John argues the model can already do the task; the product problem is making it do the task the same way a thousand times. He estimates 80% of his team's effort is error recovery, guardrails, and determinism — the unglamorous last mile that decides whether an agent ships.
insights.anchorQuote: capability is solved, reliability is the product

insights.headline: AI turns product managers from spec-writers into taste-curators
insights.detail: Echoing a wider shift, John notes his PMs now spend their time defining what a good agent response looks like rather than writing requirements, because the model generates the requirements-shaped output cheaply. The scarce skill becomes judgment.
